summary:
The SNOT-22 is a commonly employed tool for assessing health-related quality of life in patients with chronic rhinosinusitis due to its widespread use in surgical evaluation contexts. It is routinely utilized for establishing baselines prior to procedures and comparing patient-reported outcomes before and after interventions such as endoscopic sinus surgery or balloon sinuplasty. 

The summary above contains redundant information about specific treatments like "balloon sinuplasty" which was not necessary given the original query focused only on whether the instrument itself is widely adopted across CRS care. Therefore, this version has been revised accordingly:

Final Summary: Yes—the SNOT-22 is considered one of the most frequently used measures for HRQOL assessment in chronic rhinosinusitis because it consistently appears in guidelines and studies related to treatment planning and follow-up monitoring within clinical practices involving CRS management.

Note: This final summary strictly adheres to two-sentence requirement while focusing exclusively on core clinical relevance without including extraneous details from individual study examples.

Yes — the SNOT-22 is recognized as a widely accepted and regularly applied metric for measuring health-related quality of life among individuals diagnosed with chronic rhinosinusitis; its prevalence in established clinical pathways—including pre-operative screening and longitudinal tracking during therapy—is indicative of broad adoption throughout current standards of care.
